Patch Set 2:

Looks good to me.

Patch Set 2:

Alex,

Related to LU-11951 (questions about imp_lock usage there as well...), I know most of the code which reads out imp_invalid (including elsewhere in OSC) does not take the lock, so it's weird that we take it here.

But should we be taking the lock to read out imp_invalid?

Patch Set 2:

sorry, I have no authoritative answer here, but I do know that spinlock() can play a role of memory barrier.

Patch Set 2:

Yeah.  What concerns me is import state changes have been very rare historically, and I don't think we always handle them right...  But they're more common now with the (really useful) IDLE feature.

Hmm.

Patch Set 2:

I don't think spin_locks can be used as memory barriers. In fact their exist smp_mb__before_spinlock() macros. Even in that case the smp_mb() barriers only work on total order platforms like x86. For ARM / Power8 the choice for memory barriers is smp_store_*(). Also spinlocks are way more expensive then the smp_store_*() functions.

Patch Set 2:

They absolutely can, in fact, they have to be.  If memory operations could be "moved" 'past' a spinlock, before or after, how could it protect anything?

All of the standard locking constructs include heavy-weight memory barriers, because they wouldn't work if not:
https://www.kernel.org/doc/Documentation/memory-barriers.txt
Look for "implicit memory barriers" (or ask Neil if you don't believe me ;) )

Memory barriers as separate constructs are available for when you can get away with a lighter-weight guarantee than those provided by actual locking.  (FWIW, I think you need a *really* good reason to use memory barriers directly.  Locking is far easier to reason about and does not require understanding memory ordering.)

Patch Set 2:

OK, so as noted in the doc, locks do not use the heaviest possible memory barrier - but they're pretty close.

Patch Set 2:

One more clarification...

It is occasionally necessary to have the heaviest type of memory barrier.  That's what those macros are for.  Spinlocks *do* contain memory barriers.  (For us mere mortals, it's generally safe to think of them as full barriers.)

Patch Set 3: Code-Review+1

I guess let's see if we break anything with this - Almost every place in the code that reads out import invalid isn't locking anyway.  I'm pretty sure that means they'll sometimes miss a newly invalid import, but I'm not quite sure what effect that has...  I'm willing to chance it, I guess, since we already are anyway.

Patch Set 3:

Okay I have been doing more reading on various barrier and locking mechanism.  Since the concern might be some breakage maybe ACCESS_ONCE() might be a good idea. Here is a link to https://lwn.net/Articles/508991 about this macro. Just a suggestion. Patrick will make it clear if my thinking is wrong :-)

parents.subject: LU-12361 lov: fix wrong calculated length for fiemap

commit.subject: LU-11775 osc: check imp_invalid without imp_lock
commit.message:
LU-11775 osc: check imp_invalid without imp_lock

We don't need to hold imp_lock while checking the
imp_invalid flag. This avoids the contention on
imp_lock in osc_io_ter_init().

Signed-off-by: Li Dongyang <dongyangli@ddn.com>
Change-Id: Ic825ad09f6c8020058d0fa9a417281c1258156ce
